Developing top-notch tools requires scale and investing aggressively over many years, which keeps newer competitors away. However, there are large competitors such as Shopify (SHOP), Block (XYZ), and Stripe.

In my view, GoDaddy has limited pricing power, but it has a strong competitive position due to the convenience it provides by offering all the tools needed to run from the simplest website to a large e-commerce platform.

Financial Overview And Tax Adjustments

As the industry matures, revenue growth declines, as shown in the chart below. But as GoDaddy becomes larger, it benefits from operating leverage, and profitability keeps improving without showing any signs of reaching its ceiling soon.

During the most recent quarter, revenues grew by 8% Y/Y, which is in line with recent years, and operating margins expanded by 340bps to a record 21.9%.

As previously stated, GoDaddy has a substantial unearned revenue balance, which is the result of the multi-year domain subscription model. The positive working capital reduces the need to issue debt, and with the improved profitability, the debt ratio has declined to 2.3x net debt to EBITDA despite limited debt repayments in recent years.

Given GoDaddy's healthy debt ratio, the company is allocating its excess cash towards share buybacks, which is an allocation capital strategy that I highly favor, since it accelerates EPS growth.

As one would expect, GoDaddy runs a capital-light business (less than $200M in PP&E) with returns on invested capital of ~20%, albeit I expect this figure to increase as goodwill depreciates and profitability improves further.

Finally, when calculating GoDaddy's fair value (more on that later), it is important to take into consideration the tax structure, since comparing historical P/E ratios would be misleading.

When GoDaddy went public in 2015, the company was organized as an Up-C to allow its previous owners to benefit from the tax losses generated when the company was not profitable (a percentage of that benefit went to Class A common shareholders also).

In late 2023, GoDaddy eliminated all Class B shares and simplified its corporate structure, resulting in over $1B in tax credits, but the tax benefits recorded in the last two years should not be expected in the future, since there are just $108.5M of unrealized tax benefits pending.

In the short term, GoDaddy's tax rate will be below 21% due to stock-based compensation ($300M last year) and the tax credits generated from R&D expenses, but the effective tax rate will increase above 15% in the future.

AI Impacts On Growth

In recent years, GoDaddy has prioritized putting AI at the center of the company by developing Airo, a suite of AI-driven tools that help its clients with domain searches, website design, logo making, marketing campaigns, and SEO optimization.

While there is no doubt these tools improve customer experience, what is the real impact on GoDaddy's financials?

One of the main uncertainties around the recent AI boom is how much of this spending will be translated into real free cash flow.

The direct impact on revenues, in my view, might be limited. Although we are still in the early days and management is experimenting with price points, the recently launched Airo Plus costs $5 a month, and its contribution to total revenues is low for now.

However, I believe there are relevant indirect impacts. For example, the average revenue per user has increased by 10% Y/Y to $230 per user, and churn rates have declined.

Internally, GoDaddy is intensively leveraging AI to drive efficiency gains. During the most recent call, the management highlighted how they are reducing coding times, which reduces costs and allows the company to deliver faster.

Also, the launch of Ask Airo in the upcoming months should reduce customer support expenses and improve onboarding for new clients.

We recently concluded a test integrating a new partner product into GoDaddy's technology stack over a matter of weeks rather than months. Remarkably, nearly 100% of the code was generated by AI and guided by a senior engineer.

This was work that would typically have required a small team for a few months.

Source: Mark McCaffrey (CFO) - Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

In my view, the development of AI tools does not necessarily accelerate revenue growth, since a portion of it depends on the number of active websites worldwide. But I do expect major tailwinds that could boost the ongoing margin expansion.

As the company reduces SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenues and secures its market-leading position by developing superior products, operating leverage should do the rest.

GoDaddy's Fair Value

Looking ahead, the management is targeting 33% EBITDA margins by 2026 (200bp expansion) and a 6-8% revenue growth, which is in line with the consensus estimates.

However, growth rates will vary between the different segments of the business.

As shown in the chart below, the number of active websites worldwide is flattening, and Metastat projects a 6.6% CAGR until 2032 in the website builder software market.

On the other hand, I expect the A&C segment to grow faster. E-commerce is showing great momentum, and based on Grand View Research estimates, the industry is expected to grow by 11.6% until 2030.

All things considered, I believe the management's revenue targets are realistic, and as the A&C segment contributes a higher percentage of revenues, margins will keep expanding.

These assumptions don't include market share gains, which, considering GoDaddy's scale, could be possible. While I will take a conservative approach and not include any market share gains or acquisitions, there is additional upside potential.

When calculating GoDaddy's free cash flow, I exclude stock-based compensation and assume an increase in tax expenses as tax rates go up in the future.

Assuming an 8.5% discount rate (10% cost of equity, 5% cost of debt, and a beta of 1.08), a 4% growth in perpetuity, GoDaddy's stock is trading below its fair value of $192 per share.

Using a conservative 20x LTM FCF exit multiple, the stock would deliver double-digit returns over the next few years.

Risks

While I consider GoDaddy an above-average business due to its market-leading position and the recurring nature of its revenues, the company is exposed to certain risks.

A significant portion of GoDaddy's clients are microbusinesses and individuals, who have higher churn than large clients. In recent years, the customer base has not expanded despite the favorable economic environment, and I expect it to decline during an economic slowdown.

Also, a cybersecurity breach could damage its reputation. Although GoDaddy states in its annual report that the company has purchased cybersecurity insurance, the coverage may not be enough to cover all the damage.

In relation to competitive threats, I believe GoDaddy has all the ingredients to retain its leading position even in the most competitive areas of the business, but it's important to take into account that the pricing power is limited.

Another risk would be antitrust litigation, such as the recent one with Entri LLC. Although the claims were dismissed before reaching the court, GoDaddy's large market share could trigger other cases in the future.

Finally, from a short-term perspective, the management expects a 50bp revenue headwind in Q4 2025 from the .co domain loss, which will be operated by a joint venture between a Colombian registrar and Team Internet Group plc (OTCQX:TIGXF)

Is The Sell-Off Justified?

Answering the question proposed at the beginning of the article, I believe the sell-off is partially justified due to the excessive valuation reached in late 2024, but it might have gone too far.

Revenue growth is indeed slowing down compared to the last decade, and I don't expect a significant boost from AI.

Nonetheless, GoDaddy is the market leader in a growing industry with attractive financials and room to expand margins.

As the A&C segment increases as a percentage of revenues and the company keeps improving efficiency with internal AI tools, the margin expansion will drive double-digit EPS growth.

GoDaddy's stock is trading at a 23% discount to fair value even when accounting for higher tax expenses and no market share gains. Therefore, I assign GoDaddy a buy rating.

data.title: Realty Income: King Of REITs Still Offers Viable Monthly Incomes
data.description:
O's Discounted Valuations Trigger Its Viable Income Thesis At ~5% Yields

We previously covered Realty Income (NYSE:O) in June 2025, discussing how it had been outperforming despite the uncertain macroeconomic environment, as observed in the low cost of capital and the debts' stable weighted average interest rates.

Combined with the strategic diversification to the EU, where cash yields were high with excellent rental recapture increase prospects over the next few years along with the diversification to data center/ gaming sectors, which had triggered the richer adj EBITDAre margins, we had believed that the stock was likely to deliver rich operational return prospects across dividend incomes/ capital appreciation ahead.

O 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating, market sentiments remain mixed surrounding REITs in general, attributed to the great uncertainties arising from the ongoing tariff war, as the inflationary pressure rises in July 2025 and the Fed turns hawkish in the recent FOMC meeting.

Given how REITs are highly sensitive to interest rates, since they typically finance their investments through debt, it is unsurprising that the sector as a whole has underperformed the wider market AI-induced rally thus far.

This is especially since O has reported a relatively narrow "spread of 181 basis points" between its FQ2'25 investments at 7.2% weighted average initial cash yield to its short-term weighted average cost of capital, compared to the 2024 average of 243 basis points and the 2019 average of 271 basis point.

These reasons may also be why the management has leaned heavily into dilutive capital raises to fund its ongoing investment cadence, as observed in the $1.26B raised in H1'25 and another $654M to be raised in H2'25.

While this strategy may be leverage neutral, it goes without saying that O's higher share count by +3.7% on a YoY basis has already been dilutive to its AFFO per share growth to $2.11 in H1'25 (+0.9% YoY) despite the robust AFFO growth to $1.89B (+6.1% YoY).

With it remaining to be seen when a pivot may occur, we believe that the REIT sector is likely to be depressed for a little longer, for so long that borrowing costs remain expensive, as the management highlights their laser focus to maintaining the annualized pro forma adjusted EBITDA of 5.5x in line with their long-term leverage target.

Otherwise, these headwinds have been temporarily well balanced by the normalizing yield curve in the short and long term interest rates, with the expanding spread at 0.37% in August 2025 against the -1.49% observed in June 2023 and 0.25% averages in 2019 reducing the risks of a potential recession in the near-term.

This is significantly aided by the sustainability of the REITS' operations and their ability to access low capital costs during an uncertain macroeconomic environment, as similarly reiterated by numerous market players in the recent Q2'25 earnings season, Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) and O included.

O's Well Diversified & Secure Assets

O has benefited from its well diversified assets across different end markets, with 90% of its Annualized Base Rents being resilient to economic downturns and/ or isolated from e-commerce pressures, as observed in its consistently high occupancy levels despite the volatile economic cycles.

The REIT has been able to report a decent rent recapture rate of 103.6% in H1'25 (-1.2 points YoY) compared to the 29 year averages of 103% as well, along with $2.54B of YTD investments (+81.4% YoY) at 7.3% initial cash yields (-0.5 points YoY).

The same has been observed in O's raised FY2025 guidance, with investment volumes of $5B (+29.5% YoY) and AFFO per share of $4.26 at the midpoint (+1.6% YoY), compared to the original numbers of $4B (+3.6% YoY) and $4.25 (+1.4% YoY) offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, respectively.

These underscore the REIT's ability to remain profitable enough to weather the uncertain macroeconomic environment, pending the normalization in borrowing costs, the potential refinancing activities at lower interest rates, and the renewed growth opportunities arising from the increased investment levels then.

For now, patience may be more prudent.

So, Is O Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

O 5Y Stock Price

Despite the underperformance over the past few years, O has had a relatively bullish uptrend support since the early January 2025 bottom, with the stock consistently charting higher highs/ lows while seemingly on its way to break out of the 2025 resistance levels of $58s/ $60s ranges in the near-term.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Given the moderating AFFO per share growth at +2.1% through FY2027, we believe that O remains reasonably valued at FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 13.71x, compared the +4.8%/ 15.69x observed over the past five years and +5%/ 17.51x over the past ten years, respectively.

O Valuations

A similar conclusion may be observed when comparing to the sector FWD Price/ AFFO median of 15.60x and O's REIT peers, including:

  • W. P. Carey (WPC) at FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 13.44x and AFFO per share growth at +3.7% through FY2027,
  • Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) at 16.99x/ +4.6%,
  • Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) at 11.97x/ +2.2%, respectively.

This development implies that O's undervaluation may be well warranted, pending further AFFO per share growth catalysts over the next few years.

Prior to that event, we urge investors to temper their expectations indeed, since the REIT's future return profile is likely to be tied to its dividend payouts, as stock also trades close to our fair-value estimates of $58.40.

This is based on the management's raised FY2025 AFFO per share guidance to $4.26 (+1.6% YoY) and the FWD AFFO/ Price valuations of 13.71x (low compared to its 10Y mean of 17.51x, albeit near to its peer group mean of 14.07).

For now, given O's TTM AFFO Payout Ratio of 75.84% and H1'25 ratio at 75.9%, we believe that its income investment thesis remains secure, significantly aided by the relatively healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 5.5x in FQ2'25, compared to its diversified REIT peers, including WPC at 5.8x, ADC at 5.2x, and GTY at 5.2x.

This is especially since the stock price moderation by -27.8% from the February 2020 top has already triggered a relatively decent future total operational return prospect at ~7.65% (against the 5Y averages of 9.48% and 10Y averages at 9.64%).

This is based on the rich forward dividend yields of 5.55% (against the 5Y averages of 4.68% and 10Y at 4.64%) and the consensus AFFO per share growth estimates at a CAGR of +2.1% (against the 5Y historical growth at +4.8% and 10Y at +5%).

As a result of the still decent and secure monthly income investment thesis despite the potentially prolonged macroeconomic normalization cadence, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the O stock here, as part of a diversified income portfolio.

data.title: Alphabet's AI Moat: Why The Market Is Undervaluing A Giant
data.description:
Thesis

Alphabet (GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) represents a strong bull case. While the market is highly focused on the regulatory and AI competition headwinds the company is facing, it may be mispricing the giant. This may be due to overlooking the company’s options for monetizing its AI models, the growth of Google Cloud, and the strength of its Search and YouTube businesses. The company currently trades at a price-to-earnings multiple of approximately 20x, which offers a discount compared to both the S&P and its peers. Furthermore, Google’s reputation for lagging in the AI race may not be deserved, due to the company’s success with monetizing its AI features and driving user engagement.

Alphabet Overview

It is easy to view Alphabet as a series of siloed businesses, but this fails to appreciate the true competitive strength that lies in its deeply connected ecosystem. The key to this system is the self-reinforcing flywheel that these segments of the business create.

Google Services

While Alphabet is involved in many different markets and ventures, Google Services remains the engine of the company. This segment includes Search, YouTube, Android, Chrome, Maps, and the Play Store. Not surprisingly, this critical part of the company generates substantial revenue and operating income, with these figures topping $220 billion in 2024. Furthermore, this is a high-margin segment that regularly posts operating margins above 35%.

These services are also the core of Alphabet’s massive user base. The company has 15 products that serve half a billion users. Six of these products, including Search, Android, YouTube, and Gmail, serve over two billion users each. This reach provides Alphabet with unique advantages when it comes to its AI ambitions. For example, the size of Alphabet’s infrastructure makes distributing new features cost less while offering cross-selling opportunities.

Google Cloud

A challenge for companies of Alphabet’s size can be finding new growth opportunities. For Alphabet, Google Cloud has become a critical source of growth. This segment of the business’s operations has achieved an annual revenue run-rate of over $50 billion, and it is growing at an impressive 32% year-over-year rate. Google Cloud has also become sustainably profitable, as it posted $2.8 billion in operating income for the first quarter of 2025.

Alphabet’s Venture Fund

The company's “Other Bets” segment acts as an internal venture capital fund that is strategically used to pursue high-risk but high-reward ventures. This portion of the company is currently generating losses that amount to over $1 billion per quarter. However, these investments represent Alphabet’s entry into developing markets that have high potential. A perfect example of this is the company’s position in Waymo, the autonomous taxi company. Despite being high-risk, these ventures represent tremendous future growth potential for the company that is difficult for markets to effectively price into the company’s value.

The Research and Development Core

The company’s research and development segment is responsible for many critical assets. DeepMind is an example of this, as it is the lab responsible for creating Google’s Gemini models. This division also acts as a crucial hub for research whose costs are absorbed by the company as a whole but whose innovations power many of the other segments of Alphabet.

The Alphabet Flywheel

When evaluating Alphabet, it is beneficial to avoid making the mistake of viewing the various segments of the company as individual parts. In reality, these various segments work to reinforce each other, which offers Alphabet a powerful advantage. More specifically, this flywheel is composed of Alphabet’s data, AI, and distribution infrastructure.

Google Services plays several roles in its strategy. It acts both as a data collection and distribution system for the company’s AI products. This data acts as a valuable source of training data for the AI models, which provides Alphabet with a quantity and quality of data that most AI companies do not possess. These improved models are then integrated into the Google Services ecosystem, which boosts the value of the products under this umbrella. Lastly, the AI products and infrastructure are packaged and licensed to enterprise clients through Google Cloud. The unique interplay of this flywheel is a powerful competitive advantage that can also be easy to underestimate.

Alphabet’s Strengths and Catalysts

Core Business Resilience and AI Success

Concerns have been raised that AI could potentially cannibalize Alphabet’s primary revenue streams. Yet, the company’s recent performance contradicts the cannibalization concerns. Data from Alphabet’s first half of 2025 showcases the resilience of the company’s core businesses along with the effectiveness of its long-term AI monetization strategy. Search and related services showed accelerating strength by posting 12% year-over-year growth during the second quarter. This was an increase from the 10% growth during the first quarter.

In another example of the company’s self-reinforcing flywheel model, its AI Overviews are proving to be highly effective. They are currently used by billions of people a month, and they add to overall search usage. Furthermore, they are monetizing at rates that are similar to traditional search. As a result, the idea of them replacing search behavior is weakening in favor of them adding to user engagement.

Alphabet’s Strategic Cloud Wins

Google Cloud is further solidifying its position as a key enabler of the company’s efforts to capture a share of the enterprise AI market. This segment’s 32% growth in the second quarter is a reminder of this segment’s potential. The number of deals that the company secured with large-scale enterprise clients has surged, as the total value of these deals for the first half of 2025 has already exceeded the total for all of 2024. As an example of the scale of these deals, Alphabet recently secured a $10 billion deal with Meta (META).

Alphabet’s Capex Catalyst

A strong signal of management’s confidence is the recent revision of its capital expenditure forecast, which was increased to $85 billion. This investment is a long-term offensive investment in the company that is partially in response to increasing customer demand.

Much of this will be dedicated to building data centers and other infrastructure to support the substantial technical demands large commercialized AI models require. While these expenditures are substantial, they will help to support the company’s strategy for years as it continues to develop more powerful AI products.

Alphabet’s Financial Engine

Alphabet’s flywheel strategy for competing in the emerging AI market requires immense resources for building data centers, acquiring GPUs, and TPUs, along with competing for world-class talent. To this end, Alphabet’s profitability and strong balance sheets are essential components for supporting the company’s AI strategy.

Cash Flow and Profitability

Alphabet’s AI flywheel has been costly to build, and it still requires constant large investments into research and development through Google DeepMind in order to remain competitive. Fortunately, Alphabet’s core business has remained extremely strong, with it generating over $115 billion in net income during the trailing twelve months. In addition to generating cash, the company is also extremely effective in terms of margin and return on equity. For net income margin, the company has a healthy 31.12% and a return on equity of 34.83%. These healthy numbers show that Alphabet has ample resources for supporting its crucial AI flywheel strategy.

A Fortress Balance Sheet

While Alphabet has found some early success in its AI strategy, the market is still competitive, and the company has yet to meaningfully distance itself from its competitors. This will require the company to sustain its current capital expenditures into AI until it has created a long-term competitive moat. As a result, its fortress balance sheet becomes even more valuable to the company.

This stability rests on the company’s $95.15 billion in total cash and its low Total Debt to Equity ratio of 11.48%. As a result, the company enjoys a comfortable cushion and strategic flexibility without having to significantly limit funding to segments of its flywheel. The ability to sustain investing in its core operations while pursuing the rapidly developing AI market is a critical strength that helps to support a bullish outlook for the company.

A Fundamental Approach to Valuing Alphabet

A discounted cash flow analysis is one way to value Alphabet’s stock, and this analysis shows that the stock may have an intrinsic value of approximately $238. Much of this rests on the premise that Alphabet’s investments in AI will help to drive accelerating growth along with expanding profitability over the company’s long term.

Key Assumptions of the DCF

In order to utilize a DCF model, there are several key assumptions that must be made. In the case of Alphabet, starting with a growth rate of 13% in 2025 and accelerating to 15% by 2027 seems reasonable given the company’s recent performance and the monetization prospects AI offers. Furthermore, this model assumes improving efficiencies and a richer product mix, leading to the EBIT margin increasing from 33.16% to 35.16% by 2027.

CapEx is another important attribute for this model, but this is a slightly complex topic given Alphabet's massive expenditures on AI infrastructure. For the model, an assumption of CapEx normalizing to 9% of revenue is used, which is reasonable given the company’s five-year average of 11.80%.

Weighted Average Cost of Capital is another key value in the model. 8.2% is a reasonable assumption for this variable, as it reflects the likelihood of Alphabet successfully executing its AI strategy. Terminal growth is another key consideration that reflects the amount the company will generate beyond the model’s timeframe. For a company of Alphabet’s size and reach, a terminal growth rate of 4.0% is reasonable, as it is above inflation while still representing the intense competition that Alphabet could face as the AI market continues to mature.

These calculations suggest a bullish future for Alphabet with substantial upside. However, a key assumption of this projection is the revenue growth and expanding margins, which will require the company’s AI strategy to be successful.

Potential Headwinds for Alphabet

Regulatory Challenges

Alphabet’s size and influence across a range of markets make it a common target for regulatory scrutiny. This has reached new heights as the company is currently facing antitrust scrutiny in both the US and Europe. In the US, the legal action is primarily targeting the company’s search monopoly. However, Europe is targeting its ad-tech, which has preliminarily been ruled in non-compliance with the Digital Markets Act. The risks from these legal threats range from a breakup of the company to hefty fines and other punitive actions against the company.

AI Disruption of its Core Business

While Alphabet is investing heavily in AI, it should be noted that AI has the potential to disrupt Alphabet’s key search and advertising businesses. In particular, AI-native search engines could fundamentally alter how users interact with these services. This could put Alphabet on the defensive, requiring it to dramatically change one of its core pillars. An example of this could be the inclusion of AI overviews into search results. These overviews increase user engagement, but they reduce the click-through rate for websites.

Intense Cloud Competition

Google Cloud’s impressive growth makes it an important part of Alphabet’s future. However, it remains third in market share within cloud computing, with Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) leading. In its efforts to gain market share, Alphabet does benefit from the fact that Google Cloud is growing faster than its rivals, but it requires ongoing investments in order to close this gap. As a result, an execution failure in this segment could pose a risk for Alphabet’s AI plans and growth opportunities.

Conclusion

The evidence strongly indicates that Alphabet’s stock is trading at a discount relative to its intrinsic value. The company’s flywheel structure for developing and distributing its AI products, along with its strong financials, gives it the resources to continue growing.
